source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 4.5.1-4.5.7 file/more/less/cat/tac/head/tail (pp. 50-53)

questions.q: Show what TYPE of file /etc/passwd is (ASCII text, directory, and so on).
questions.why: file reports the type of the file(s) given, e.g. 'ASCII text' or 'directory'.

questions.q: Display the whole contents of /etc/passwd to standard output in one go.
questions.why: cat concatenates the given files and writes them to standard output.

questions.q: You are reading a file with 'less'. Which key quits back to the prompt?
questions.why: less is interactive; q or Q quits. Inside it, /text searches, Space pages down, g and G jump to first and last page.
cat -n numbers all output lines; -b would number only the non-empty lines.

questions.q: What is the difference between 'cat -n' and 'cat -b'?
options: -n numbers all lines; -b numbers only non-empty lines
-n numbers only non-empty lines; -b numbers all lines
-n numbers backwards; -b numbers from the bottom
questions.why: -n numbers every line, -b skips blank lines when numbering.

questions.q: What does the tac command do, and why is it handy for logs?
options: It prints a file starting from the last line, so the newest log entries appear first
It prints only the first line of a file
It counts the lines in a file
questions.why: tac is roughly cat in reverse - it shows a file from the end, useful for reading the most recent log lines first.

questions.q: Display just the first 5 lines of /etc/passwd.
questions.why: head -n x shows the first x lines; with no -n it defaults to the first 10 lines.
tail -n x shows the last x lines; without -n it shows the last 10.

questions.q: Follow the file /var/log/messages, showing new lines as they are written in real time.
questions.why: tail -f keeps printing changes to the file until you press Ctrl+C - the standard way to watch a log live.
